conditions: Registry persists only the definition layer, never live working memory or transcripts, so per-run provenance stays certifiable.
Each run clones a version-pinned definition into a fresh ephemeral instance cleaned up at run-end exactly as in v0.3.
Cross-run context is admitted only via an explicit, signed, opt-in 'lessons' artifact, never auto-injected, so 'no contamination by default' holds.
content.keyRisk: Staleness + cross-run contamination via the registry, plus lifecycle/GC complexity for versioning and cleaning stale entries.
content.position: Hybrid: a persistent registry stores reusable expert DEFINITIONS (persona, tuning, opt-in distilled lessons) and warm-starts run-scoped ephemeral instances whose working state is still deleted at run-end to keep the zero-residue gate.
wouldChangeIf: Definition-layer reuse cannot be cleanly separated from working-state reuse, collapsing the isolation boundary.
Version-pinning fails to make registry-warm-started runs reproducible against pure ephemeral runs.

conditions: v0.3 cleanup remains a hard certification gate that proves zero residue after every run.
Cold-start cost is bounded by deterministic projection from a versioned, content-addressed source at session start.
Context that must survive a run is written to an explicit, auditable artifact store OUTSIDE the expert, not carried as hidden in-expert state.
content.keyRisk: Cold-start cost and loss of accumulated context: re-projecting every run adds latency and discards warm context unless an external auditable context store offsets it.
content.position: Ephemeral: project experts per run from a versioned source and delete them at run-end; zero cross-run contamination, simple lifecycle, run-scoped provenance the runtime can certify.
wouldChangeIf: A persistent registry demonstrates certifiable zero cross-run contamination at equal or lower audit cost than ephemeral cleanup.
Cold-start latency is measured to dominate total run cost AND cannot be mitigated by deterministic projection/caching of immutable source.

counterpoints: Contamination via lessons -> separate signed artifact, off by default, explicit recorded hash pin only.
content.position: Hybrid: registry holds only content-addressed, version-pinned expert DEFINITIONS; each run warm-starts ephemeral, run-scoped INSTANCES deleted at run-end, preserving the zero-residue gate.
proposals: Two-layer model: REGISTRY = DEFINITIONS only; RUNTIME = ephemeral run-scoped INSTANCES deleted at run-end; gate unchanged.
Content-addressed definitions: identity = hash(content); provenance records definition_hash per expert.
Signed opt-in lessons artifact for cross-run context, OFF by default (no lessons => identical to pure ephemeral).
Staleness controls: TTL + supersedes; expired hashes refused unless force-pinned. Registry GC removes only UNREFERENCED hashes.
content.reasoning: Ephemeral conflates the DEFINITION (instructions/role/tools/slice — expensive, slow-to-stabilize) with the INSTANCE (cheap, must-be-clean). Because custom agents load only at session start, the registry is a build-time input, not a live shared object: instances still spin up fresh and die at run-end. Content-addressing makes definition identity = its bytes, so a run records exactly which hash it used — provenance is STRONGER than a free-form per-run 'versioned source' that can drift silently. Nothing mutable crosses runs: definitions immutable-by-hash, instances deleted, learning only via an explicit signed opt-in artifact.

references.comment: Steel-man then counter: ephemeral conflates DEFINITION with INSTANCE; immutable hashed definitions add warm-start without mutable cross-run state.
Extends my declaration with the content-addressed + signed-opt-in-lessons mechanism.

counterpoints: Cold-start latency is the strongest hit against ephemeral; mitigated (not eliminated) by a digest-keyed cache.
Losing accumulated context is real; answer is explicit signed promotion to the source, which costs ceremony.
content.position: Ephemeral per-run experts: deterministically project each expert from a versioned, content-addressed source at run-start and delete at run-end. Zero contamination by default, trivial lifecycle, fully certifiable run-scoped provenance.
proposals: Ephemeral default: project at run-start from a content-addressed source pinned by digest; delete instances at run-end.
Replace 'persistent registry' with an immutable digest-keyed projection CACHE: warm-start = cache hit on unchanged source digest.
Promotion-by-commit: any 'learned' improvement is an explicit signed versioned change to the source (reviewable diff).
Certification scope check: require the gate to attest the SOURCE DIGEST consumed per run.
content.reasoning: Steel-man: the hybrid (r1-msg-001) is the strongest opposing design; the definition/instance split is real and warm-start value is real. Counter: certification is about what the gate can SEE and ATTEST. The moment ANY definition persists across runs you introduce mutable cross-run state with three surfaces the run-scoped gate cannot observe: (1) staleness drift — an edited/auto-updated definition makes run N+5 silently differ from run N; (2) contamination via the definition channel — 'definitions' is exactly where accumulated context leaks, so residue moves from the instance layer the gate certifies to the definition layer it does not; (3) supply-chain — a long-lived mutable registry is a higher-value target. Decisive point: a digest-pinned source plus an immutable projection CACHE already buys most warm-start benefit; a hash-addressed cache is reconstructible and self-certifying.

references.comment: Concede the split and warm-start value, but the persisted DEFINITION layer is mutable cross-run state the gate cannot see; the zero-residue claim covers instance deletion, not definition drift.
How does the gate certify a definition was unchanged since the last attested run without re-deriving its digest?
Extends the ephemeral position: digest-pinned source + immutable projection cache recovers most warm-start latency without a mutable persisted layer.

content.failureScenario: A pinned, validly-signed expert definition is reused unchanged across runs; meanwhile the dependency lockfile and underlying model are upgraded. definition_hash is identical, the commit diff is empty, the zero-residue gate passes — yet the expert now behaves differently and silently imports stale assumptions. Contamination returns through the door everyone agreed to lock, and nothing in the certified scope can see it.
content.loadBearingAssumption: 'Same bytes + valid signature => same safe behavior in the next run.' True for bytes, false for behavior: a hash certifies the definition's REPRESENTATION, not its EFFECT when composed with this run's inputs, drifted dependencies, or a changed model. Hash stability — the proof of safety both cite — is exactly what is blind to semantic contamination.
content.provesMeWrong: Show the gate attests SEMANTICS, not just bytes: each cross-run reuse re-runs the definition against a behavioral conformance/contamination suite pinned to THIS run's inputs, dependency digests, and model ID, failing closed on divergence even when definition_hash is unchanged, plus a machine-enforced expiry/re-attestation on the opt-in so it cannot silently drift on.
content.targetConsensus: Both experts converged on the belief that content-addressing + immutability + signed opt-in makes cross-run reuse SAFE and CERTIFIABLE (r1-msg-003 hashed definitions; r1-msg-004 digest-pinned cache + promotion-by-commit).
content.weakestEvidence: r1-msg-004's 'reviewable diff' is EMPTY precisely when a definition is byte-identical across runs, so the human review surface for the most dangerous case (stale-but-unchanged) is zero. And r1-msg-003's 'off by default' is a human gate that erodes under deadline pressure.

references.comment: definition_hash + signed opt-in certifies bytes, not behavior re-composed with a new run; 'off by default' is a human gate that erodes.
The promotion-by-commit 'reviewable diff' is empty exactly for byte-identical reuse — the stale-but-unchanged case gets the least review.
Sharpens the ephemeral claim: certify 'zero byte residue + run-scoped provenance', not 'zero semantic contamination'.

content.currentPosition: Hybrid registry with content-addressed definitions, but reuse keyed on a machine-enforced run-context fingerprint with TTL/re-attestation that fails closed on drift; human opt-in carryover removed.
content.position: Hybrid registry remains correct, but content-addressing must bind DEFINITION bytes to a run-context fingerprint (inputs + dependency digests + model id) with machine-enforced expiry and fail-closed re-attestation; bytes alone are necessary but not sufficient for semantic identity.
content.previousPosition: Hybrid registry with content-addressed version-pinned definitions plus signed opt-in lessons off by default.
proposals: Effective identity = H(definition_hash || sorted(dependency_digests) || model_id || input_schema_digest); cache/warm-start key on this composite.
Machine-enforced expiry/re-attestation; on mismatch/expiry the run fails CLOSED and re-projects.
Carryover ONLY as signed promotion-by-commit folded into the fingerprinted source digest; drop 'off by default' opt-in.
content.reasoning: Conceding r1-msg-005: a content hash certifies bytes not behavior; byte-identical reuse under upgraded deps/model drifts silently and the promotion diff is empty, while 'off by default' is an erodable human gate. The fix is to widen the identity unit into a composite run-context fingerprint so any dep/model/input change mints a NEW effective identity, forcing re-projection or re-attestation — converting the human gate into a machine gate while keeping warm-start.
content.shiftReason: r1-msg-005 showed byte-level content addressing + the zero-residue gate cannot detect semantic drift when deps/model change beneath a stable hash, and 'off by default' is erodable; moved to composite fingerprint identity + machine-enforced fail-closed re-attestation.

references.comment: Concede: hashes certify bytes not semantics; the byte-identical promotion diff is empty; 'off by default' erodes.
Promotion-by-commit becomes the SOLE carryover path, folded into the fingerprinted source digest.
Zero-residue inspects carried state and cannot detect drift where state is unchanged but deps/model moved.

content.currentPosition: Ephemeral per-run experts with the certified claim narrowed to zero-byte-residue + run-scoped provenance; reuse bound to a run-context fingerprint and failing closed on drift even when the content hash is unchanged.
content.position: Ephemeral per-run experts remain correct, but the honest claim narrows to what the runtime can certify: zero BYTE residue + run-scoped provenance — never 'zero semantic contamination.' The rule certifies only what the runtime can see and binds every reuse to a run-context fingerprint, failing closed on drift even when the content hash is unchanged.
content.previousPosition: Ephemeral projection from a digest-pinned source with an immutable projection cache, treating content-hash identity as sufficient for safe reuse.
proposals: Narrow the certified claim to 'zero BYTE residue + run-scoped provenance'; disclaim 'zero semantic contamination' as outside runtime attestation.
run-context fingerprint = hash(input slice IDs + dependency digests + model id + projection-source digest); reuse valid ONLY when the fingerprint matches.
Fail closed on any fingerprint mismatch; emit the fingerprint as run-scoped provenance so audits distinguish byte-identity from behavioral-identity.
content.reasoning: r1-msg-005 lands: content hashes certify bytes not semantics, byte-identical reuse under upgraded deps/model drifts silently, the reviewable diff is empty, and 'off by default' erodes. I accept the narrowing. Ephemerality still guarantees no MUTABLE state crosses runs and gives run-scoped provenance the runtime can attest; make the runtime's observable signal the binding key and treat the unobservable as a drift risk that must fail closed.
content.shiftReason: r1-msg-005 showed content hashes certify bytes not semantics, byte-identical reuse yields an empty diff, and 'off by default' erodes — so hash-identity is not a safe reuse certificate. Narrowed the honest claim and re-keyed reuse on a full run-context fingerprint that fails closed on drift the hash cannot see.

references.comment: Accepts the byte-vs-semantics gap; converts silent drift into a fail-closed event keyed on a run-context fingerprint.
My earlier 'immutable projection cache recovers warm-start' was too strong: cache validity must key on the full fingerprint, not the source digest alone.
Keeps ephemeral projection + zero contamination, but restates the guarantee as byte-residue + provenance rather than semantic.

positionShifts.from: Hybrid registry with content-addressed version-pinned definitions plus signed opt-in lessons off by default (nothing-mutable-crosses-runs treated as sufficient).
positionShifts.reasoning: Conceded to the contrarian (r1-msg-005) that byte-level content addressing and the zero-residue gate cannot see semantic drift when deps/model change beneath a stable hash, and that 'off by default' is an erodable human gate; folded the skeptic's promotion-by-commit (r1-msg-004) into a fingerprinted source digest.
positionShifts.to: Hybrid registry, but reuse keyed on a machine-enforced run-context fingerprint (definition digest + inputs + dependency digests + model id) with TTL/re-attestation that fails closed on drift; human opt-in carryover removed.

positionShifts.from: Ephemeral projection from a digest-pinned source with an immutable projection cache, treating content-hash identity as sufficient for safe reuse.
positionShifts.reasoning: Accepted the contrarian's narrowing (r1-msg-005): hash-identity is not a safe-reuse certificate, so certify only what the runtime can see and re-key reuse on a full run-context fingerprint that fails closed on drift the hash cannot see.
positionShifts.to: Ephemeral default with the certified claim narrowed to zero-byte-residue + run-scoped provenance; any reuse bound to a run-context fingerprint and failing closed on drift even when the content hash is unchanged.

activeDisagreements.point: Default posture: registry-by-default warm-start (advocate) vs ephemeral-by-default with reuse only as a fingerprinted exception (skeptic).

positions.stance: hybrid registry warm-start as the normal path

activeDisagreements.point: Whether fingerprint-bound reuse can ever be allowed for a certification-gated run, or certified runs must always project fresh.

positions.stance: certified runs may reuse if fingerprint matches

agreements.point: Run-scoped provenance and zero cross-run contamination by default are non-negotiable; the v0.3 instance-deletion gate stays.
Certify only what the runtime can attest; the honest claim is 'zero byte residue + run-scoped provenance', not 'zero semantic contamination'.
Any reuse must be content-addressed AND bound to a run-context fingerprint that fails closed on drift; human 'off by default' opt-in is replaced by a machine gate.

decision.choice: Hybrid, but ephemeral-by-default: project + delete each run (v0.3 zero-residue gate unchanged); allow OPTIONAL content-addressed reuse only as a machine-gated exception bound to a run-context fingerprint.
decision.oneRule: An expert may be reused across runs ONLY when bound to a run-context fingerprint = H(definition/persona digest + input slice ids + dependency digests + model id) that the runtime attests and that FAILS CLOSED on any mismatch; otherwise project fresh and delete. Reuse is an explicit, machine-enforced exception, never the default.
decision.topRisk: Semantic staleness the residue/digest gates cannot see: a byte-identical, validly-signed definition silently drifts under upgraded deps/model (empty review diff), and fail-closed fingerprinting can trigger re-projection storms / cold-start cost. A clean fingerprint still does not bound nondeterministic model-sampling drift.

insights.insight: The ephemeral-vs-registry axis dissolves into a definition/instance split; the real boundary is byte-identity vs behavioral-identity.
A content hash certifies representation, not effect; the most dangerous reuse (stale-but-unchanged) produces an EMPTY review diff, so human gates are weakest exactly where contamination hides.
Binding reuse to a run-context fingerprint (deps + model + inputs) converts an erodable human gate into a machine gate that fails closed.

minorityReport.note: Un-refuted by argument: the advocate conceded fingerprinting is necessary but did not show certified-run reuse is ever safe; recorded as a live dissent, not resolved by majority.
minorityReport.position: Stay ephemeral-by-default: reuse must be OFF by default and only ever a machine-gated fingerprinted exception; certified runs should project fresh.
minorityReport.reason: Even a fail-closed fingerprint cannot bound nondeterministic model-sampling drift, and any default-on reuse path erodes under deadline pressure; the only fully-certifiable posture is project-and-delete.

openQuestions: Which dependency digests are behavior-relevant enough to gate the fingerprint without causing needless cold re-projection?
How is nondeterministic model-sampling drift bounded when the fingerprint is unchanged?
What cold-start latency budget justifies a warm-start (reuse) exception over fresh projection?
synthesis.recommendationReason: Genuine disagreement was engineered (counter/questions edges + a contrarian stress pass), both experts shifted, and a concrete, certifiable decision rule emerged; further rounds would refine fingerprint scope, not change the decision.
synthesis.summary: Advocate (hybrid registry) and skeptic (ephemeral) started genuinely opposed, then both shifted (minor) under a contrarian stress test that the consensus 'content-addressing + signed opt-in makes reuse safe/certifiable' certifies BYTES, not SEMANTICS. They converged on ephemeral-by-default with reuse permitted only when bound to a fail-closed run-context fingerprint.

topic: Should swarm-discussion experts be ephemeral per-run or a persistent registry?
